schools.summary: Samson telling Delilah all his heart, that his strength lay in his unshorn Nazarite hair: 'that he told her all his heart, and said unto her, There hath not come a razor upon mine head; for I have been a Nazarite unto God from my mother's womb: if I be shaven, then my strength will go from me, and I shall become weak, and be like any other man' — the surrender of the secret of consecration — 'that he told her all his heart... I have been a Nazarite unto God from my mother's womb: if I be shaven, then my strength will go from me': Samson reveals that his strength is bound up with his Nazarite vow and its sign, the unshorn hair (the witnesses noting the significance — the fatal disclosure by which Samson surrendered to his betrayer the secret of his strength, which lay not in the hair itself but in his consecration to God of which the hair was the appointed sign, so that to part with the hair was to break the vow and forfeit the presence and power of God, in telling 'all his heart' he gave away the very ground of his calling, the sign of his separation profaned, the soul's defence surrendered to the enemy, the strength resting on the consecration, not the hair, the betrayal of the secret the betrayal of the vow, the heart wholly opened to the destroyer, the lesson — that to surrender the secret of our consecration, to give the heart's stronghold to the world that flatters us, is to forfeit our strength and lay ourselves open to ruin, 'keep thy heart with all diligence; for out of it are the issues of life,' Proverbs 4:23, the truth that the believer's strength lies in his separation to God, and that to profane the sign and break the vow is to lose the power, 'if a man purge himself he shall be a vessel unto honour,' 2 Timothy 2:21, the figure — the soul that yields its consecration to the enemy and so loses its power, fulfilled in every Christian who, betraying his heart to the world, forfeits his spiritual strength, 'ye did run well; who did hinder you?' Galatians 5:7, the truth that the sign without the substance is nothing, but to cast away the sign is to renounce the substance, the figure of the betrayal of the secret of consecration, the fatal disclosure that surrenders the soul's defence to the enemy, the call to guard the secret of our communion with God, the warning that to open the heart to the world's flattery is to lose our strength); Samson telling all his heart, revealing that his strength was bound up with his Nazarite consecration of which the unshorn hair was the sign (the heart opened, Proverbs 4:23) — the disclosure (told her all his heart); 'if I be shaven, then my strength will go from me, and I shall become weak, and be like any other man' (Samson's strength lay not in the hair but in the consecration it signified, so that to be shaven was to break the vow and forfeit the power of God, the believer's strength in his separation to God, 2 Timothy 2:21; Galatians 5:7) — the figure (the soul that yields its consecration to the enemy and so loses its power, the providence, the disclosure, Delilah calling the lords and shaving him to follow, vv.18-19) — Keil and Delitzsch, Calvin, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: Wherein lay Samson's strength, and what was betrayed?

sides.position: The secret of consecration was betrayed - his strength lay not in the hair itself but in his consecration to God of which the unshorn hair was the appointed sign, so that to be shaven was to break the vow and forfeit the power of God, the soul's defence surrendered to the enemy, the believer's strength lying in his separation to God (Proverbs 4:23; 2 Timothy 2:21; Galatians 5:7)

themes: That he told her all his heart, and said unto her, There hath not come a razor upon mine head; for I have been a Nazarite unto God from my mother's womb: if I be shaven, then my strength will go from me, and I shall become weak, and be like any other man
Samson telling Delilah all his heart, that his strength lay in his unshorn Nazarite hair (the crux of the betrayal of the secret of consecration)

the fatal disclosure by which Samson surrendered the secret of his strength, which lay not in the hair but in his consecration of which the hair was the appointed sign, so that to part with it was to break the vow and forfeit the power of God

the soul's defence surrendered to the enemy, the believer's strength lying in his separation to God, the soul that betrays its heart to the world losing its power (Proverbs 4:23; 2 Timothy 2:21; Galatians 5:7)

schools.summary: Delilah shaving off Samson's hair, his strength departing: 'and she made him sleep upon her knees; and she called for a man, and she caused him to shave off the seven locks of his head; and she began to afflict him, and his strength went from him' — Samson's hair was shaved and his strength departed (the and she made him sleep upon her knees; and she called for a man, and she caused him to shave off the seven locks of his head, the shaving, the and she began to afflict him, and his strength went from him, the strength gone, the figure, the sign of consecration cut off and the power withdrawn, the vow broken, the truth that with the shaving of his hair the broken vow lost him the power of God, Numbers 6:7, the figure of the strength that departs when the consecration is profaned, the power withdrawn, the shaving of Samson, the strength gone, vv.17); Delilah shaving off his locks (the vow broken, Numbers 6:7) — the shaving (the seven locks); 'his strength went from him' (the power of God withdrawn with the broken vow, the strength departed, the figure, the strength that departs when the consecration is profaned, the providence, the shaving, the LORD departing and Samson taken to follow, vv.20-21) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And she made him sleep upon her knees; and she called for a man, and she caused him to shave off the seven locks of his head; and she began to afflict him, and his strength went from him

schools.summary: Samson awaking to fight as before, not knowing the LORD had departed from him: 'and she said, The Philistines be upon thee, Samson. And he awoke out of his sleep, and said, I will go out as at other times before, and shake myself. And he wist not that the LORD was departed from him' — the lost presence unperceived — 'and he awoke out of his sleep, and said, I will go out as at other times before, and shake myself. And he wist not that the LORD was departed from him': Samson, presuming on a strength that was gone, did not know that the LORD had left him (the witnesses noting the significance — the most tragic and solemn word in the whole history, that Samson, his hair shorn and his vow broken, rose confident as ever and meant to act as before, never suspecting that the secret of his strength, the presence of the LORD, had silently withdrawn, the source of his power gone while he presumed it remained, the awful self-deception of one who has lost God and does not feel the loss, the strength was never in the hair but in the LORD whose presence the hair betokened, the departure silent and unnoticed, the presumption of acting in a strength that is no longer there, the lesson — that a man may lose the presence and power of God and not be aware of it, going through the old motions of duty and conflict while the secret of his strength is gone, 'Ephraim, he hath mixed himself among the people... strangers have devoured his strength, and he knoweth it not,' Hosea 7:8-9, the truth that the worst loss is the loss we do not perceive, the soul that has grieved away the Spirit yet presumes upon his help, 'thou knowest not that thou art wretched, and miserable, and poor, and blind,' Revelation 3:17, the figure — the backslidden soul that retains the form while the power is fled, fulfilled in every professor who, having grieved the Spirit, presumes to serve and finds himself powerless, 'having a form of godliness, but denying the power thereof,' 2 Timothy 3:5, the truth that the presence of God, not any outward sign, is the secret of all spiritual strength, and that to lose it unawares is the deepest peril, the figure of the tragedy of presumption, the soul that has lost the presence of God yet does not know it, the call to watch lest we grieve away the Spirit and not perceive it, the warning that we may presume upon a strength that has silently departed); Samson rising confident as ever, meaning to act as before, presuming on a strength already gone (the old motions in a lost power, Hosea 7:8-9) — the presumption (as at other times); 'And he wist not that the LORD was departed from him' (the LORD had silently departed, the source of his strength gone while Samson presumed it remained, the soul that has lost God and does not feel the loss, the form retained while the power is fled, the presence withdrawn unawares, Revelation 3:17; 2 Timothy 3:5) — the figure (the backslidden soul that retains the form while the power is fled, the providence, the loss, the Philistines seizing and blinding Samson to follow, vv.21) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'he wist not that the LORD was departed from him' reveal?

sides.position: The tragedy of presumption, the soul that has lost the presence of God yet does not know it - Samson rising confident as ever, never suspecting that the presence of the LORD, the true secret of his strength, had silently withdrawn, a man able to lose the power of God and not perceive it (Hosea 7:8-9), the worst loss the loss we do not feel (Revelation 3:17), the form retained while the power is fled (2 Timothy 3:5)

schools.summary: The Philistines blinding Samson and setting him to grind in prison: 'but the Philistines took him, and put out his eyes, and brought him down to Gaza, and bound him with fetters of brass; and he did grind in the prison house' — Samson, his strength gone, was blinded, bound, and set to grind (the but the Philistines took him, and put out his eyes, the blinding, the and brought him down to Gaza, and bound him with fetters of brass; and he did grind in the prison house, bound and grinding, the figure, the mighty deliverer brought to blindness and slavery, the wages of sin paid, the truth that Samson, who followed his eyes into sin, lost them, and the strong man ground at the mill, Proverbs 5:22, the figure of the bondage and shame that sin brings on the fallen, the captive of his own lust, the captivity of Samson, the blind grinding, vv.20); Samson blinded by the Philistines (the judgment, Proverbs 5:22) — the blinding (put out his eyes); 'bound him with fetters of brass; and he did grind in the prison house' (the strong man bound and grinding, the slavery, the figure, the bondage and shame that sin brings on the fallen, the providence, the captivity, his hair beginning to grow again to follow, vv.22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Samson's hair beginning to grow again after he was shaven: 'howbeit the hair of his head began to grow again after he was shaven' — Samson's hair, and with it the sign of his returning consecration, began to grow (the howbeit the hair of his head began to grow again after he was shaven, the hair growing, the figure, the sign of consecration renewed, the hope of restored strength as the heart returned to God, the truth that the growing hair betokened the returning of God's favour as Samson repented, Psalm 30:5, the figure of the mercy that revives the fallen, the renewal of consecration and hope, the hair renewed, the hope of restoration, vv.17); the hair growing again, the sign of returning consecration (the renewal, Psalm 30:5) — the renewal (the hair grew); 'after he was shaven' (the hair growing after the shaving, the hope of restored strength, the renewal, the figure, the mercy that revives the fallen, the providence, the renewal, the Philistines gathering to mock Samson to follow, vv.23) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The Philistines gathering to sacrifice to Dagon, crediting him with Samson's capture: 'then the lords of the Philistines gathered them together for to offer a great sacrifice unto Dagon their god, and to rejoice: for they said, Our god hath delivered Samson our enemy into our hand' — the Philistines ascribed their victory to their idol Dagon (the then the lords of the Philistines gathered them together for to offer a great sacrifice unto Dagon their god, and to rejoice, the feast to Dagon, the for they said, Our god hath delivered Samson our enemy into our hand, the false triumph, the figure, the idolaters crediting their false god with the work, the blasphemy of the triumph, the truth that the Philistines blasphemously ascribed Samson's fall to Dagon, Daniel 5:4, the figure of the false worship that glories over God's fallen servant, the idol exalted, the feast to Dagon, the false triumph, vv.21); the Philistines feasting to Dagon (the idolatry, Daniel 5:4) — the feast (sacrifice to Dagon); 'Our god hath delivered Samson our enemy into our hand' (the victory ascribed to Dagon, the blasphemy, the figure, the false worship that glories over God's fallen servant, the providence, the triumph, Samson brought out to make sport to follow, vv.25) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The people praising Dagon at the sight of the captive Samson: 'and when the people saw him, they praised their god: for they said, Our god hath delivered into our hands our enemy, and the destroyer of our country, which slew many of us' — the people gave Dagon the glory for Samson's defeat (the and when the people saw him, they praised their god, the praise of Dagon, the for they said, Our god hath delivered into our hands our enemy, and the destroyer of our country, which slew many of us, the glory to the idol, the figure, the multitude joining the blasphemy, the idol glorified over the fallen servant, the truth that the people ascribed the victory to Dagon, glorying over Samson, Psalm 115:4, the figure of the world that glories over the humbled people of God, the idol praised, the praise of the people, the glory to Dagon, vv.23); the people praising Dagon (the idolatry, Psalm 115:4) — the praise (praised their god); 'Our god hath delivered into our hands our enemy, and the destroyer of our country, which slew many of us' (the glory given to the idol, the blasphemy, the figure, the world that glories over the humbled people of God, the providence, the praise, Samson called to make sport to follow, vv.25) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The Philistines calling for Samson to make them sport between the pillars: 'and it came to pass, when their hearts were merry, that they said, Call for Samson, that he may make us sport. schools.summary: Samson asking to feel the pillars to lean on them: 'and Samson said unto the lad that held him by the hand, Suffer me that I may feel the pillars whereupon the house standeth, that I may lean upon them' — Samson asked to be set where he might grasp the pillars (the and Samson said unto the lad that held him by the hand, Suffer me that I may feel the pillars whereupon the house standeth, the request to feel the pillars, the that I may lean upon them, to lean on them, the figure, the blind deliverer reaching for the pillars of his vengeance, the design hidden, the truth that Samson sought the pillars to pull down the house, Judges 16:29, the figure of the purpose of God working through the blind man's request, the vengeance prepared, the request of Samson, the pillars sought, vv.25); Samson asking to feel the pillars (the design, Judges 16:29) — the request (feel the pillars); 'that I may lean upon them' (the pretext of leaning, the design hidden, the vengeance prepared, the figure, the purpose of God working through the blind man's request, the providence, the request, Samson's last prayer to follow, vv.28) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The house full of Philistines and lords beholding Samson's sport: 'now the house was full of men and women; and all the lords of the Philistines were there; and there were upon the roof about three thousand men and women, that beheld while Samson made sport' — the whole multitude and the lords were gathered to mock him (the now the house was full of men and women; and all the lords of the Philistines were there, the throng and the lords, the and there were upon the roof about three thousand men and women, that beheld while Samson made sport, the multitude on the roof, the figure, the whole foe gathered for the mockery, ripe for the judgment they did not see, the truth that the multitude assembled to mock would perish together, Job 21:30, the figure of the foe gathered for triumph and gathered for judgment, the harvest of vengeance ripe, the throng of Philistines, the multitude doomed, vv.25); the house full and the lords present (the assembly, Job 21:30) — the throng (full of men and women); 'there were upon the roof about three thousand men and women, that beheld while Samson made sport' (the three thousand on the roof, doomed, the multitude gathered, the figure, the foe gathered for triumph and for judgment, the providence, the throng, Samson's last prayer and the pulling down to follow, vv.28-30) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Samson's last prayer, that God would remember and strengthen him once more: 'and Samson called unto the LORD, and said, O Lord GOD, remember me, I pray thee, and strengthen me, I pray thee, only this once, O God, that I may be at once avenged of the Philistines for my two eyes' — the faith of the restored penitent — 'and Samson called unto the LORD, and said, O Lord GOD, remember me, I pray thee, and strengthen me, I pray thee, only this once': Samson, humbled and penitent, casts himself on God for one last enabling (the witnesses noting the significance — the prayer of a soul restored, the fallen Nazarite returning to the God he had grieved, owning his dependence wholly on the LORD, 'strengthen me,' pleading God's remembrance and mercy rather than his own desert, 'remember me,' and seeking strength not in his hair or himself but in God alone, the faith that brings the penitent back to the source of his strength, in this prayer Samson is numbered among the faithful, his last act an act of faith, the strength sought from God, not presumed, the penitent's plea grounded in mercy, the restored servant casting himself on God, the lesson — that the fallen who repent may return to God and find him ready to hear, the source of strength still open to the contrite, 'the LORD is nigh unto them that are of a broken heart,' Psalm 34:18, the truth that true prayer owns its dependence wholly on God and pleads his mercy, not our merit, 'have mercy upon me, O God, according to thy lovingkindness,' Psalm 51:1, the figure — the penitent restored to faith and power, Samson named among the heroes of faith for this last act, 'Gedeon... Samson... out of weakness were made strong,' Hebrews 11:32-34, the truth that God hears the cry of the returning soul and renews its strength, the figure of the penitent's return to God in faith, the fallen servant casting himself again on the mercy and power of the LORD, the call to return to God in our falls and cast ourselves on his mercy, the assurance that God hears the penitent and renews his strength); Samson casting himself wholly on God, seeking strength from him alone and pleading his remembrance and mercy, the penitent's return to the source of his strength (the dependence on God, Psalm 34:18) — the prayer (remember me, strengthen me); 'O God, that I may be at once avenged of the Philistines for my two eyes' (Samson seeking, as God's appointed judge, vengeance on the Philistine enemies of God and his people, his cause God's cause, the last act of faith numbering him among the faithful, the judge's vengeance on God's foes, Psalm 51:1; Hebrews 11:32-34) — the figure (the penitent restored to faith and power, the providence, the prayer, the pulling down of the house to follow, vv.29-30) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Samson's last prayer reveal?

sides.position: The penitent's return to God in faith, the fallen servant casting himself again on the mercy and power of the LORD - the prayer of a soul restored, owning his dependence wholly on the LORD and pleading God's mercy not his own desert, seeking strength in God alone, the fallen who repent finding God ready to hear (Psalm 34:18; Psalm 51:1), Samson numbered among the heroes of faith, out of weakness made strong (Hebrews 11:32-34)

schools.summary: Samson grasping the two pillars of the house: 'and Samson took hold of the two middle pillars upon which the house stood, and on which it was borne up, of the one with his right hand, and of the other with his left' — Samson laid hold of the supporting pillars (the and Samson took hold of the two middle pillars upon which the house stood, and on which it was borne up, the pillars grasped, the of the one with his right hand, and of the other with his left, right and left, the figure, the restored strength laying hold of the means of vengeance, the answer to the prayer, the truth that Samson, his strength restored, grasped the pillars to pull down the house, Judges 16:28, the figure of the renewed power of God in the penitent, the strength returned, the grasp of Samson, the pillars laid hold of, vv.28); Samson grasping the supporting pillars (the strength restored, Judges 16:28) — the grasp (the two middle pillars); 'of the one with his right hand, and of the other with his left' (the pillars held in both hands, the strength returned, the figure, the renewed power of God in the penitent, the providence, the grasp, the pulling down and Samson's death to follow, vv.30) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Samson's death, pulling down the house so that he slew more in his death than in his life: 'and Samson said, Let me die with the Philistines. And he bowed himself with all his might; and the house fell upon the lords, and upon all the people that were therein. So the dead which he slew at his death were more than they which he slew in his life' — the victory through death — 'and Samson said, Let me die with the Philistines. And he bowed himself with all his might; and the house fell upon the lords... So the dead which he slew at his death were more than they which he slew in his life': Samson, willing to die, pulls down the house and gains his greatest victory in death (the witnesses noting the significance — the crowning act of Samson's life, his greatest deliverance wrought not in his strength but in his death, willingly giving his own life to destroy the enemies of God and his people, so that his dying blow accomplished more than all his living exploits, the lords and the multitude of the Philistines perishing with him, in this self-sacrifice the deliverer conquers by dying, his death the means of the greatest overthrow of the foe, the willing death for the people, the victory in the moment of seeming defeat, more slain in death than in life, the lesson — that God can make the death of his servant the means of his greatest triumph, the seeming defeat the true victory, 'except a corn of wheat fall into the ground and die, it abideth alone: but if it die, it bringeth forth much fruit,' John 12:24, the truth that the greatest victories of God's kingdom are often won through suffering and death, not power, the figure — the great Deliverer who, by his own death, destroyed the enemy and wrought the greatest deliverance, Samson a dim type of Christ who through death overcame him that had the power of death, 'that through death he might destroy him that had the power of death,' Hebrews 2:14, the truth that the death of the willing servant accomplishes more than his life, the cross the place of victory, the figure of the victory wrought through the death of God's servant, the deliverer who conquers the foe by dying with them, the call to count no sacrifice too great for God's cause, the assurance that God turns the death of his servants into triumph); Samson willingly giving his life to destroy the enemies of God and his people, bowing with all his might and pulling down the house (the willing death for the people, John 12:24) — the death (let me die with the Philistines); 'So the dead which he slew at his death were more than they which he slew in his life' (Samson's dying blow slew more than all his living exploits, the victory wrought through death, a dim type of Christ who through death destroyed the power of death, the victory in death, Hebrews 2:14) — the figure (the great Deliverer who by his own death destroyed the enemy, the providence, the death, the burial of Samson by his brethren to follow, vv.31) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Samson slaying more in his death than in his life signify?

sides.position: The victory wrought through the death of God's servant, the deliverer who conquers the foe by dying with them - the greatest deliverance wrought not in his strength but in his death, willingly giving his life so his dying blow accomplished more than all his living exploits, God making the death of his servant his greatest triumph (John 12:24), a dim type of Christ who through death destroyed him that had the power of death (Hebrews 2:14)

themes: And Samson said, Let me die with the Philistines. And he bowed himself with all his might; and the house fell upon the lords, and upon all the people that were therein. So the dead which he slew at his death were more than they which he slew in his life
